content: The refractive indices of glass and quartz w.r.t. air are 3/2 and 12/5 respectively. The refractive index of quartz w.r.t. glass is
The radius of curvature of a concave mirror is 24 cm and the image is magnified by 1.5 times. The object distance is
explanation:
$$R = - 24$$ cm, $$f = - 12$$ cm, $$m = 1.5$$

$${1 \over v} + {1 \over u} = {1 \over f}$$;

$${1 \over {1.5\,u}} + {1 \over u} = - {1 \over {12}}$$

$${{2.5} \over {1.5\,u}} = - {1 \over {12}}$$

or, $$u = - 20$$ cm

$$-$$ve sign show that object is placed in front of a convex mirror.

content: A point source of light is kept at a depth of h in water of refractive index 4/3. The radius of the circle at the surface of water through which light emits is
explanation:
$${{\sin 90^\circ } \over {\sin C}} = \mu $$

$$\sin C = {1 \over \mu } \Rightarrow {R \over {\sqrt {{R^2} + {h^2}} }} = {3 \over 4}$$

Squaring, $$16{R^2} = 9{R^2} + 9{h^2}$$

$$7{R^2} = 9{h^2} \Rightarrow R = {3 \over {\sqrt 7 }}\,h$$

content: Figure shows the graph of angle of deviation $\delta$ versus angle of incidence i for a light ray striking a prism. The prism angle is
explanation:
We know that,

$$ \Rightarrow \quad \begin{aligned} & \delta=i+e-A \\ & A=i+e-\delta \\ & =60+15-30=45^{\circ} \end{aligned} $$

content: Two convex lens $$(\mathrm{L}_1$$ and $$\mathrm{L}_2)$$ of equal focal length $$\mathrm{f}$$ are placed at a distance $$\frac{\mathrm{f}}{2}$$ apart. An object is placed at a distance $$4 \mathrm{f}$$ in the left of $$\mathrm{L_1}$$ as shown in figure. The final image is at
explanation:
$\begin{aligned} & L_1: \frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{-4 f}=\frac{1}{f} \\\\ & \frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{f}-\frac{1}{4 f}=\frac{4-1}{4 f} \\\\ & v=\frac{4 f}{3}\end{aligned}$

$L_2: u=\frac{4 f}{3}-\frac{f}{2}=\frac{8 f-3 f}{6}=\frac{5 f}{6}$

$\begin{array}{r}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f} \\\\ \frac{1}{v}-\frac{6}{5 f}=\frac{1}{f} \\\\ \frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{f}+\frac{6}{5 f}=\frac{5+6}{5 f} \\\\ v=\frac{5 f}{11} \Rightarrow \text { right side of } L_2\end{array}$

content: If $$\hat{n}_1, \hat{n}_2$$ and $$\hat{\mathrm{t}}$$ represent, unit vectors along the incident ray, reflected ray and normal to the surface respectively, then
explanation:
Angle of incidence $=\theta$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \hat{n}_1=\cos \theta(-\hat{\mathrm{j}})+\sin \theta(\hat{\mathrm{i}}) \\\\ & \hat{n}_2=\cos \theta(\hat{\mathrm{j}})+\sin \theta(\hat{\mathrm{i}}) \\\\ & \hat{n}_1 \cdot \hat{\mathrm{t}}=\cos \left(180^{\circ}-\theta\right)=-\cos \theta \\\\ & \hat{n}_1-\hat{n}_2=-2 \cos \theta(\hat{\mathrm{j}})=-2 \cos \theta \hat{\mathrm{t}}=+2\left(\hat{n_1} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{t}}\right) \hat{\mathrm{t}} \\\\ & \hat{n}_2=\hat{n}_1-2\left(\hat{n}_1 \cdot \hat{\mathrm{t}}\right) \hat{\mathrm{t}} \end{aligned} $$

content: When a convex lens is placed above an empty tank, the image of a mark at the bottom of the tank, which is 45 cm from the lens is formed 36 cm above the lens. When a liquid is poured in the tank to a depth of 40 cm, the distance of the image of the mark above the lens is 48 cm. The refractive index of the liquid is
explanation:
Here we will apply the lens formula and concept of refractive index.

By this formula,

$${1 \over v} - {1 \over u} = {1 \over f}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over {36}} - {1 \over { - 45}} = {1 \over f}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{5 + 4} \over {180}} = {1 \over f}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {9 \over {180}} = {1 \over f}$$

$$ \Rightarrow f = 20$$ cm

Now, when tank is filled with water,

$$u = - \left( {5 + {{40} \over \mu }} \right)$$

By lens formula,

$${1 \over v} - {1 \over u} = {1 \over f}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over {48}} + {1 \over {5 + {{40} \over \mu }}} = {1 \over {20}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\mu \over {5\mu + 40}} = {1 \over {20}} - {1 \over {48}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\mu \over {5\mu + 40}} = {{12 - 5} \over {240}} = {7 \over {240}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 240\mu = 35\mu + 280$$

$$ \Rightarrow 205\mu = 280$$

$$ \Rightarrow \mu = {{280} \over {205}} = {{56} \over {41}} = 1.366$$

Hence, $$\mu=1.366$$

content: Three identical convex lenses each of focal length $$\mathrm{f}$$ are placed in a straight line separated by a distance $$\mathrm{f}$$ from each other. An object is located at f/2 in front of the leftmost lens. Then,

options.content: Final image will be at f/2 behind the rightmost lens and its magnification will be $$-1$$.
Final image will be at f/2 behind the rightmost lens and its magnification will be $$+1$$.
Final image will be at f behind the rightmost lens and its magnification will be $$-1$$.
Final image will be at f behind the rightmost lens and its magnification will be $$+1$$.

explanation:
For Lens $$L_1 \quad u=-\frac{f}{2}, f=+f$$

$$\therefore \quad \frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{-\frac{f}{2}}=\frac{1}{f}$$

$$\therefore \quad v=-f$$ and $$m=v / u=2$$

For Lens $$L_2 \quad u=-2 f, v=2 f$$

$$\therefore \quad m=\frac{v}{u}=-1$$

For Lens $$L_3 \quad u=+f, f=+f$$

$$\therefore \quad \frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{f} \Rightarrow v=\frac{f}{2}$$

and $$\quad m=\frac{v}{u}=\frac{1}{2}$$

$$\therefore \quad m=m_1 \cdot m_2 \cdot m_3=2 \times(-1) \times \frac{1}{2}=-1$$

Hence, final image will be $$\frac{f}{2}$$ behind the lens $$L_3$$ and the magnification is $$-$$1.

content: The human eye has an approximate angular resolution of $$\theta$$ = 5.8 $$\times$$ 10$$-$$4 rad and typical photo printer prints a minimum of 300 dpi (dots per inch, 1 inch = 2.54 cm). At what minimal distance d should a printed page be held so that one does not see the individual dots?
explanation:
Given, angular resolution of human eye,

$$\theta =5.8\times 10^{-4}$$ rad

The linear distance between two successive dots in a printer,

$$l = {{2.54} \over {300}} = 0.846 \times {10^{ - 2}}$$ cm

At a distance $$d$$ cm, the gap $$l$$ will subtend an angle which is given by

$$d = {l \over \theta } = {{0.846 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}} \over {5.8 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}} = 14.59$$ cm

content: A spherical convex surface of power 5 D separates object and image space of refractive indices 1.0 and $$4\over3$$ , respectively. The radius of curvature of the surface is
explanation:
The situation is as shown in the figure below


Given,

P = 5 D

$$\therefore$$ $$f = {1 \over P} = {1 \over 5}$$ m = 20 cm

Let u = $$-$$ $$\infty$$ (object distance)

Then, v = f (by sign convention)

Using formula for refraction at spherical surface,

$${{{\mu _2}} \over v} - {{{\mu _1}} \over u} = {{{\mu _2} - {\mu _1}} \over R}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {4 \over {3 \times (20)}} - {1 \over {( - \infty )}} = {{{4 \over 3} - 1} \over R}$$ [$$\because$$ v = 20 cm]

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over {15}} = {1 \over {3R}}$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ R = 5 cm

content: The cross-section of a reflecting surface is represented by the equation x2 + y2 = R2 as shown in the figure. A ray travelling in the positive x-direction is directed toward positive y-direction after reflection from the surface at point M. The coordinate of the point M on the reflecting surface is
explanation:
The given situation is shown below


Here, i = r

Since, i + r = 90$$^\circ$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ i + i = 90$$^\circ$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ i = 45$$^\circ$$

i = r = 45$$^\circ$$

Coordinate at point P is

(R cos$$\theta$$, R sin$$\theta$$) = (R cos135$$^\circ$$, R sin135$$^\circ$$)

$$ = \left[ {R\left( {{{ - 1} \over {\sqrt 2 }}} \right),R\left( {{1 \over {\sqrt 2 }}} \right)} \right]$$

$$ = \left( {{{ - R} \over {\sqrt 2 }},{R \over {\sqrt 2 }}} \right)$$

content: A tennis ball hits the floor with a speed v at an angle $$\theta $$ with the normal to the floor. If the collision is inelastic and the coefficient of restitution is $$\varepsilon $$, what will be the angle of reflection?
explanation:
Given, a tennis ball hits the floor with a speed v making an angle $$\theta $$ with the normal as shown in the figure below.


The coefficient of restitution = $$\varepsilon $$

Now, from the above diagram,

In $$\Delta AOB,\tan \phi = {{v\sin \theta } \over {\varepsilon v\cos \theta }} = {{\tan \theta } \over \varepsilon }$$

$$ \therefore $$ Angle of reflection, $$\phi = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {{{\tan \theta } \over \varepsilon }} \right)$$

content: An object, is placed 60 cm in front of a convex mirror of focal length 30 cm. A plane mirror is now placed facing the object in between the object and the convex mirror such that it covers lower half of the convex mirror. What should be the distance of the plane mirror from the object, so that there will be no parallax between the images formed by the two mirrors?
explanation:
The given situation is as shown in figure below.


Object distance from convex mirror, u = $$ - $$ 60 cm

Focal length of convex mirror, f = 30 cm

Using mirror formula,

$${1 \over v} + {1 \over u} = {1 \over f} \Rightarrow {1 \over v} - {1 \over {60}} = {1 \over {30}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over v} = {1 \over {30}} + {1 \over {60}} = {{2 + 1} \over {60}} = {3 \over {60}}$$

$$v = 20$$ cm

Distance between the object and image = 60 + 20 = 80 cm

As there is no parallex between the image formed by the two mirrors, i.e. the images are formed at the same place.

So, the plane mirror is at a distance of 40 cm from the object.

content: A thin convex lens is placed just above an empty vessel of depth 80 cm. The image of a coin kept at the bottom of the vessel is thus formed 20 cm above the lens. If now water is poured in the vessel upto a height of 64 cm, what will be the approximate new position of the image? Assume that refractive index of water is 4/3.
explanation:
A thin convex lens is placed just above an empty vessel of depth 80 cm. The image (I) of a coin kept at the bottom of the vessel is thus formed 20 cm above the lens. If now water is poured in the vessel upto a height of 64 cm as shown in the figure below.


Given, object distance, u = $$ - $$ 80 cm

Image distance, v = + 20 cm

Using lens formula, we get

$${1 \over f} = {1 \over v} - {1 \over u}$$

$${1 \over f} = {1 \over {20}} + {1 \over {80}} = {5 \over {80}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ f = 16 cm

Now, water is poured in the vessel upto height of 64 cm, then object distance,

$$u = 16 + {{64} \over \mu } = 16 + {{64 \times 3} \over 4} = 64$$ cm

(given, refractive index, $$\mu $$ = 4/3)

Again, using lens formula,

$${1 \over f} = {1 \over v} - {1 \over u}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $${1 \over {16}} = {1 \over v} + {1 \over {64}}$$ .......($$ \because $$ u = $$ - $$ 64 cm)

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over v} = {3 \over {64}}$$

$$ \therefore $$ v = 21.33 cm, above the lens.

content:
A ray of light is reflected by a plane mirror. $${\widehat e_0}$$, $$\widehat e$$ and $$\widehat n$$ be the unit vectors along the incident ray, reflected ray and the normal to the reflecting surface respectively.

Which of the following gives an expression for $$\widehat e$$?
explanation:
According to the question,


We know that incident ray, reflected ray and normal lie in the same plane.

and angle of incidence = angle of reflection

Therefore, $$\widehat n$$ will be along the angle bisector of $$\widehat e$$ and $$-$$$$\widehat e$$0,

i.e. $$\widehat n = {{\widehat e + ( - {{\widehat e}_0})} \over {|\widehat e - {{\widehat e}_0}|}}$$ ... (i)

[$$ \because $$ Bisector will along a vector dividing in same ratio as the ratio of sides forming that angle]

But $$\widehat n$$ is a unit vector where $$|\widehat e - {\widehat e_0}| = OC$$

$$ = 2OP = 2|\widehat e|$$

$$\cos \theta = 2\cos \theta $$

Substituting this value in Eq. (i) , we get

$$\widehat n = {{\widehat e - {{\widehat e}_0}} \over {2\cos \theta }}$$

$$\widehat e = {\widehat e_0} + (2\cos \theta )\widehat n$$

$$\widehat e = {\widehat e_0} - 2(\widehat n\,.\,{\widehat e_0})\widehat n$$ [$$ \because $$ $$\widehat n\,.\,{\widehat e_0} = - \cos \theta $$]

content:
A point object is placed on the axis of a thin convex lens of focal length 0.05 m at a distance of 0.2 m from the lens and its image is formed on the axis. If the object is now made to oscillate along the axis with a small amplitude of A cm, then what is the amplitude of oscillation of the image?

[you may assume, $${1 \over {1 + x}} \approx 1 - x$$, where x < < 1]
explanation:
According to the question, we can draw the following diagram


Given, u = $$-$$0.2 m and f = 0.05 m

As we know that,

$${1 \over f} = {1 \over v} - {1 \over u}$$ ...(i)

$${1 \over v} = {1 \over f} + {1 \over u}$$

$${1 \over v} = {1 \over {0.05}} - {1 \over {0.2}}$$

$${1 \over v} = {{100} \over 5} - {{10} \over 2}$$

$${1 \over v} = 20 - 5 \Rightarrow v = {1 \over {15}}$$ m

Now, differentiating eq. (i), we get

$$ - {{dv} \over {{v^2}}} = - {{du} \over {{u^2}}}dv = du{{{v^2}} \over {{u^2}}}$$

$${A_{\max }} = A \times {\left( {{1 \over {15}}} \right)^2} \times {\left( {{1 \over {( - 0.2)}}} \right)^2}$$

$${A_{\max }} = A \times {1 \over {225}} \times 25{A_{\max }} = {A \over 9}$$

Here A is in cm.

Hence, $${A_{\max }} = {A \over 9} \times {10^{ - 2}}$$ m

content: A point source is placed at coordinates (0, 1) in xy-plane. A ray of light from the source is reflected on a plane mirror placed along the X-axis and perpendicular to the xy-plane. The reflected ray passes through the point (3, 3). What is the path length of the ray from (0, 1) to (3, 3)?
explanation:
Ray diagram for the question,


I is image of source S by the plane mirror placed perpendicularly along X-axis.

$$ \because $$ SM = IM

$$ \therefore $$ PM + MS = PM + MI = PI

PI = $$\sqrt {{{(3 - 10)}^2} + {{(3 + 1)}^2}} $$

$$ = \sqrt {9 + 16} = \sqrt {25} = 5$$

content: Two identical equiconvex lenses, each of focal length f are placed side by side in contact with each other with a layer of water in between them as shown in the figure. If refractive index of the material of the lenses is greater than that of water, how the combined focal length F is related to f?
explanation:
The given combination of lenses


Here, f1 = focal length of equiconvex lenses of glass. f2 = focal length of lens formed by water (concave). The focal length of the combination.

$$ \therefore $$ $${1 \over F} = {1 \over {{f_1}}} + {1 \over {{f_2}}} + {1 \over {{f_3}}} = {1 \over {{f_1}}} - {1 \over {{f_2}}} + {1 \over {{f_1}}} = {2 \over {{f_1}}} - {1 \over {{f_2}}}$$

$${1 \over F} = {{2{f_2} - {f_1}} \over {{f_1}{f_2}}} \Rightarrow F = {{{f_1}{f_2}} \over {2{f_2} - {f_1}}}$$ [$$ \because $$ f3 = f1]

$$ = F = {{{f_1}} \over {2 - {{{f_1}} \over {{f_2}}}}}$$ .... (i)

Here, $${1 \over {{f_1}}} = ({\mu _g} - 1){2 \over R}$$, for L1 and L3

and $${1 \over {{f_2}}} = ({\mu _w} - 1)\left( { - {2 \over R}} \right)$$, for L2

Given, $${\mu _w} < {\mu _s}$$

Thus, $${{{f_1}} \over {{f_2}}} < 1$$

So, $$F > {{{f_1}} \over 2}$$ .... (ii)

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$${{{f_1}} \over 2} < F < {f_1}$$ or $${{{f_1}} \over 2} < F < f$$ ($$ \because $$ f1 = f)

content: There is a small air bubble at the centre of a solid glass sphere of radius r and refractive index $$\mu$$. What will be the apparent distance of the bubble from the centre of the sphere, when viewed from outside?
explanation:
As the object is at centre of the sphere.

$$ \therefore $$ All rays will fall normally on surface, hence they do not deflect. Thus, a virtual image is formed at the centre O.

$$ \therefore $$ Apparent depth = Real depth

content: The ratio of the diameter of the sun to the distance between the earth and the sun is approximately 0.009. The approximate diameter of the image of the sun formed by concave spherical mirror of radius of curvature 0.4 m is
explanation:
The angle subtended by sun or mirror

$$Q = {D \over {{d_{SE}}}} = {d \over f}$$


$$ \therefore $$ $$d = {D \over {{d_{SE}}}} \times f$$

According to the question,

$${D \over {{d_{SE}}}} = 0.009$$

$$f = {r \over 2} = {{0.4} \over 2} = 0.2$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$d = 0.009 \times 0.2$$ m

$$ = 9 \times 2 \times {10^{ - 4}}$$ m

$$ = 18 \times {10^{ - 4}}$$ m

$$ = 1.8 \times {10^{ - 3}}$$ m

content: A point object is held above a thin equiconvex lens at its focus. The focal length is 0.1 m and the lens rests on a horizontal thin plane mirror. The final image will be formed at

options.content: 0.1 m above the center of the lens
0.1 m below the center of the lens

explanation:
As the lens is placed on a horizontal thin plane mirror.


So due to role of plane mirror the image will be formed at the same point O above 0.1 m distance above the center of the lens.

content: A parallel beam of light is incident on a glass prism in the shape of a quarter cylinder of radius R = 0.05 m and refractive index n = 1.5, placed on a horizontal table as shown in the figure. Beyond the cylinder, a patch of light is found whose the nearest distance x from the cylinder is
explanation:
Given, radius R = 0.05 m

Refractive index n = 1.5


The critical angle, $$\sin c = {1 \over n} = {1 \over {1.5}}$$

$$ = {{10} \over {15}} = {2 \over 3}$$

From above figure,

$$\cos c = {R \over {R + X}}$$

$$\sqrt {1 - {{\sin }^2}c} = {R \over {R + X}}$$

$$\sqrt {1 - {4 \over 9}} = {R \over {R + X}}$$

$${{\sqrt 5 } \over 3} = {R \over {R + X}}$$

$$\sqrt 5 (R + X) = 3R$$

$$\sqrt 5 R + \sqrt 5 X = 3R$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$X = (3\sqrt 5 - 5) \times {10^{ - 2}}$$ m

content: A ray of light strikes a glass plate at an angle of 60$$^\circ$$. If the reflected and refracted rays are perpendicular to each other, the refractive index of glass is
explanation:
Here, angle of incidence is 60$$^\circ$$



$$\mu = {{\sin i} \over {\sin r}}$$

$$\sin i = \mu \sin r$$

here, $$r = 90 - i$$

$$\sin i = \mu \sin (90^\circ - i) = \mu \cos i$$

$$\mu = {{\sin i} \over {\cos i}} = \tan i = \tan 60^\circ $$

$$\mu = \sqrt 3 $$

content: Light travels through a glass plate of thickness t and having refractive index $$\mu$$. If c be the velocity of light in vacuum, time taken by the light to travel through this thickness of glass is
explanation:
Thickness of glass plate = t

Refractive index = $$\mu$$

Velocity of light = c

Speed of light = $${{dis\tan ce} \over {time}} = {t \over T}$$

T = $${t \over {speed\,of\,light}}$$

Speed of light in glass plate

$$v = {c \over \mu }$$ ($$ \because $$ $$\mu = {c \over v}$$)

$$T = {t \over {c/\mu }} = {{\mu t} \over c}$$

content: A ray of light is incident on a right angled isosceles prism parallel to its base as shown in the figure. Refractive index of the material of the prism is $$\sqrt 2 $$. Then, which of the following statement(s) is/are true?

options.content: The reflection at P is total internal.
The reflection at Q is total internal.
The ray emerging at R is parallel to the ray incident at S.
Total deviation of the ray is 150$$^\circ$$.

explanation:
Critical angle $${\theta _c} = {\sin _{ - 1}}\left( {{1 \over {\sqrt 2 }}} \right) = 45^\circ $$

Snell's law at points, ($${n_1}\sin {\theta _1} = {n_2}\sin {\theta _2}$$)

$$1\sin 45^\circ = \sqrt 2 \sin \theta $$

$$\theta = 30^\circ $$

At P $$ \because $$ incidence angle at $$P = 75^\circ > {\theta _c}$$

$$ \therefore $$ TIR at point P.

At Q $$ \because $$ incidence angle at $$Q = 15^\circ > {\theta _c}$$.

$$ \therefore $$ Partial reflection and refraction at Q.

As SP || QR, emergent ray at R is parallel to incident ray as S.

$$ \therefore $$ Net deviation = 180$$^\circ$$.
